section.title: You Will Not Abandon My Soul
section.sectionRationale: One of the Psalter's most explicitly messianic texts, quoted at length by Peter at Pentecost and by Paul at Pisidian Antioch as direct prophecy of Christ's resurrection. David voices a settled security in God as his whole portion, and his closing confidence that God will not abandon him to Sheol reaches beyond what David's own body experienced, pointing to the Holy One who would not see decay.
section.thesis: David's confession that the Lord is his only good and his secure portion rises, in its final verses, to a hope beyond his own grave, a hope the apostles show is fulfilled only in the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: David's refuge and the delight he takes in the saints rather than false gods; the Lord as his chosen portion and secure inheritance; his confidence that God is always before him so he will not be shaken; and the climactic hope that God will not abandon his soul to Sheol, fulfilled in Christ's resurrection.

units.label: Apart from You I have no good thing
units.note: refuge and delight in the saints

verses.text: Preserve me, O God, for in You I take {{chasah|refuge}}.
I said to the LORD, “You are my Lord; apart from You I have no good thing.”

As for the saints in the land, they are the excellence 
in whom all my delight resides.
Sorrows will multiply to those who chase other gods. I will not pour out their libations of blood, or speak their names with my lips.

units.label: The Lord is my chosen portion

verses.text: The LORD is my chosen {{cheleq|portion}} and my cup; You have made my lot secure.
The lines of my boundary have fallen in pleasant places; surely my inheritance is delightful.
I will bless the LORD who counsels me; even at night my conscience instructs me.
I have set the LORD always before me. Because He is at my right hand, I will not be shaken.
Therefore my heart is glad and my tongue rejoices; my body also will dwell securely.

units.label: You will not abandon my soul to Sheol

verses.text: For You will not abandon my soul to {{sheol|Sheol}}, nor will You let Your {{chasid|Holy One}} see decay.

You have made known to me the path of life; You will fill me with joy in Your presence, 
with eternal pleasures at Your right hand.

chasah.gloss: To take refuge, seek shelter, flee for protection. David opens the psalm by casting himself on God as his only place of safety, the same posture of trust that runs through the whole psalm's confidence.

cheleq.gloss: Portion, share, allotted inheritance, drawn from the language of Israel's tribal land divisions. David says the LORD Himself, not a plot of ground, is his cheleq, the secure share that defines his whole life.

sheol.gloss: The realm of the dead, the grave, the place of departed spirits in Old Testament thought. David's confidence that God will not abandon him to Sheol reaches for a hope beyond death itself, one the New Testament shows fulfilled in resurrection.

chasid.gloss: Holy One, godly one, one marked by covenant loyalty and faithfulness. Peter and Paul both apply this title in verse 10 directly to Jesus Christ, whose body, unlike David's, never saw decay, since He rose bodily from the dead.

basic.context: David opens by casting himself entirely on God for refuge, delighting in God's people rather than in idols. He then declares that the Lord Himself, not land or wealth, is his chosen portion and secure inheritance. Because he keeps the Lord always before him, he will not be shaken, and his confidence rises to its highest point in the final verses: God will not abandon his soul to the grave, nor let His Holy One see decay, and he will know the fullness of joy in God's presence forever.
basic.hermeneutics: David wrote this psalm from his own experience of trust in God, and in one sense it does describe his own settled hope. But the apostles Peter and Paul both insist, quoting verse 10 directly, that its ultimate fulfillment goes beyond David: David himself died and was buried and his body did see decay, so this verse points forward, as genuine prophecy, to the resurrection of great David's greater Son, Jesus Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: David chasah, takes refuge, in God alone, and calls the LORD his cheleq, portion, the secure share that replaces any claim to land or wealth. His climactic hope is that God will not abandon him to sheol, the realm of the dead, nor let His chasid, Holy One, see decay, language the apostles apply directly to Christ's bodily resurrection.
basic.typology: David, God's anointed king, functions here as a type of the greater Anointed One, Christ, whose body alone truly never saw decay; David's own hope in this psalm reaches further than his own experience could fulfill, anticipating the resurrection accomplished in Jesus.
basic.christ: In Acts 2:25-31, Peter preaches at Pentecost that David could not have been speaking only of himself in verse 10, since David's tomb was in Jerusalem to that very day; David, as a prophet, spoke of the resurrection of the Christ. Paul repeats this same argument in Acts 13:35-37 at Pisidian Antioch, declaring that Jesus, unlike David, saw no decay because God raised Him from the dead.
basic.god: God is shown as the sole sufficient good, the secure portion, and counselor who guides even in the night, and ultimately as the One whose faithfulness reaches beyond death itself, refusing to abandon His Holy One to the grave.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 2:25-31 is Peter's Pentecost sermon quoting this psalm as prophecy of Christ's resurrection. Acts 13:35-37 is Paul's parallel argument at Pisidian Antioch. Hebrews 2:11-13 shows Christ delighting in His people much as David delights in the saints.
basic.oneStory: David's confession that God alone is his portion and refuge rises to a hope beyond his own grave, a hope the whole rest of the story shows was never meant for David alone but for the greater Son of David, Jesus Christ, whose resurrection secures eternal joy in God's presence for everyone who trusts in Him.

context.literary: A psalm of trust structured in an ascending movement, from refuge and delight in God's people, through the language of secure inheritance, to a climactic hope reaching beyond death, titled in Hebrew a miktam, likely a term for a treasured or memorable composition.
context.historical: Attributed to David, likely written during a season when he had personally renounced the worship of other gods common among the nations around Israel, choosing instead to find his whole portion in the LORD alone.
context.canonical: Among the clearest direct messianic prophecies quoted in the New Testament, cited at length in the two great missionary sermons of Acts, Peter's at Pentecost and Paul's at Pisidian Antioch, both making resurrection from the dead central to the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express David's settled trust that God alone is his sufficient good and secure inheritance, and to voice, by the Spirit's inspiration, a hope in resurrection life that reached beyond what David himself would experience.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's personal confession of trust; its prescriptive and doctrinal weight, confirmed by apostolic interpretation, is that it is genuine messianic prophecy of the resurrection of Christ, not merely a devotional expression of general hope.

debates.question: Did David understand himself to be prophesying the resurrection of a future Messiah when he wrote verse 10, or did he simply express confident hope in God's care of his own life?

views.label: David spoke better than he knew, uttering a genuine prophecy of Christ's resurrection under the Spirit's inspiration, as the apostles explicitly teach
views.case: Peter states plainly in Acts 2:30-31 that David, being a prophet, foresaw and spoke of the resurrection of the Christ; this is the clear apostolic reading and the historic Christian interpretation.

views.label: David expressed personal confidence that God would preserve his life and not let him die prematurely or in disgrace, language later shown by the apostles to have a deeper, fuller meaning in Christ
views.case: The psalm's immediate language of refuge, portion, and security throughout fits David's own circumstances; its full resurrection meaning becomes clear only in light of its New Testament application, a pattern sometimes called sensus plenior, a fuller sense intended by the divine author.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm, following the apostles, that the ultimate and truest fulfillment of verse 10 is the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ, since David's own body did see decay; the question concerns how fully David consciously understood this as he wrote, not whether Christ is the fulfillment.

study.originalLanguages: David casts himself in chasah, refuge, on God alone, naming Him his cheleq, portion, in place of any earthly inheritance. His hope climaxes in confidence that God will not abandon him to sheol, the grave, nor let His chasid, Holy One, see decay, a promise the apostles show finds its full and true fulfillment only in the risen Christ.

typology.type: David, God's anointed, confident of preservation from the grave, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, the truly Holy One, whose body did not see decay because God raised Him bodily on the third day, Acts 2:31.

typology.type: The path of life and fullness of joy in God's presence, verse 11
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection life and eternal joy secured for believers through union with the risen Christ, Hebrews 12:2.

study.christ: Peter's Pentecost sermon in Acts 2:25-31 quotes Psalm 16:8-11 directly and argues that David, as a prophet who knew God had sworn to seat one of his descendants on his throne, foresaw and spoke of the resurrection of the Christ, since David's own body remained in the tomb and saw decay, while Jesus did not. Paul repeats the same argument in Acts 13:35-37, making this psalm central to the earliest apostolic preaching of the resurrection.
study.god: God is David's sole sufficient good, his portion and secure inheritance, his counselor even in the night, and ultimately the One whose covenant faithfulness triumphs over death itself, refusing to abandon His Holy One to the grave.

crossReferences.note: Peter's Pentecost sermon quotes this psalm directly as prophecy fulfilled in Christ's resurrection.
Paul makes the identical argument at Pisidian Antioch, that Jesus, unlike David, saw no decay.
Shows Christ, like David in this psalm, delighting in and identifying with His people, the saints in the land.

study.oneStory: David's confession that God alone is his refuge and portion climbs, in its final verses, to a hope beyond the grave that his own body could not fulfill. The apostles show that hope was always meant for great David's greater Son, whose bodily resurrection secures for all who trust Him the very path of life and eternal joy this psalm describes.

sermon.title: Prayer for Security
sermon.why: Keller preaches this psalm as the very text Peter used in the first Christian sermon at Pentecost, tracing how David's cry for an unshakeable refuge is answered fully only in the risen Christ.

quiz.q: What does David declare in verse 2 about his relationship to God?

options.text: That the LORD is his Lord, and apart from Him he has no good thing.
options.note: This is David's direct confession of total dependence on God as his sole good.

options.text: That he has many good things apart from God that still bring him joy.
options.note: This directly contradicts David's stated confession in verse 2.

options.text: That other gods provide some lesser but real good things.
options.note: David explicitly rejects other gods in verse 4, warning that sorrows multiply for those who chase them.

options.text: That good things come primarily from his own wisdom and effort.
options.note: This shifts credit away from God as the sole source, which is the opposite of what David confesses.

quiz.q: What does David mean by calling the LORD his 'portion' in verse 5?

options.text: That God Himself, not land or wealth, is his secure and chosen inheritance.
options.note: This draws on the language of Israel's tribal land divisions, applying it to God Himself as David's true inheritance.

options.text: That God has given him a specific plot of land in Israel.
options.note: The psalm uses inheritance language metaphorically for God Himself, not a literal land grant.

options.text: That his financial portion is secured through wise investment.
options.note: The psalm concerns spiritual security in God, not financial planning.

options.text: That he shares his portion equally among his household.
options.note: This misses the point that the portion described is God Himself, not a distributed inheritance.

quiz.q: How does Peter use Psalm 16:10 in his Pentecost sermon in Acts 2?

options.text: He argues that David, as a prophet, foresaw and spoke of the resurrection of the Christ, since David's own body remained in the tomb and saw decay.
options.note: This is Peter's exact argument in Acts 2:25-31, central to the first Christian sermon.

options.text: He argues that David's own body was miraculously preserved from decay.
options.note: Peter explicitly states the opposite, that David's tomb was still present and his body did see decay.

options.text: He uses it only as a general example of Old Testament poetry.
options.note: Peter's use is a specific theological argument for Christ's resurrection, not merely a poetic illustration.

options.text: He applies it to the future resurrection of all believers only, not to Jesus specifically.
options.note: Peter's argument centers specifically on Jesus's own resurrection as the fulfillment, though it has implications for believers as well.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's climactic hope in verses 10-11?

options.text: That God will not abandon his soul to Sheol nor let His Holy One see decay, and will show him the path of life and fullness of joy in His presence.
options.note: This is the psalm's highest point of hope, quoted by the apostles as messianic prophecy.

options.text: That David will avoid all suffering in his earthly life.
options.note: The psalm's hope concerns life beyond death, not the absence of earthly suffering.

options.text: That his descendants will inherit great wealth.
options.note: The psalm's climax concerns resurrection hope, not material inheritance for descendants.

options.text: That his enemies will be publicly humiliated.
options.note: This is not the psalm's focus in these verses, which center on personal hope beyond the grave.

quiz.q: Why is Psalm 16 significant for the earliest Christian preaching?

options.text: Both Peter at Pentecost and Paul at Pisidian Antioch quote verse 10 directly as scriptural proof of Christ's bodily resurrection.
options.note: This makes Psalm 16 one of the most explicitly messianic and apostolically confirmed prophetic texts in the Psalter.

options.text: It is quoted only once, briefly, with little theological weight given to it.
options.note: It is quoted at length in two major sermons, Acts 2 and Acts 13, each making it central to the argument for the resurrection.

options.text: It was primarily used by early Christians as a song of comfort with no doctrinal significance.
options.note: While it is comforting, its explicit doctrinal use by the apostles as resurrection proof is highly significant, not merely devotional.

options.text: It has no connection to the resurrection accounts in the Gospels or Acts.
options.note: This is directly contradicted by its prominent use in both Acts 2 and Acts 13.

questions: David says apart from God he has 'no good thing'. Where are you tempted to look for your ultimate good in something other than God, even something good in itself?

David calls the LORD his chosen portion and secure inheritance rather than land or wealth. What would change in your priorities this week if you truly held God, not your possessions or achievements, as your portion?
David sets the LORD always before him so that he will not be shaken. What practices help you keep God consciously before you in ordinary moments, not just in crisis?

This psalm's hope of resurrection was fulfilled specifically in Jesus, not in David himself. How does knowing Christ's resurrection is a settled historical fact, not just a future hope, change the way you face your own mortality?
The psalm ends with 'fullness of joy' and 'eternal pleasures' in God's presence. How does anticipating that promised joy shape the way you endure hardship or delayed gratification now?

translationNotes.note: Refuge renders chasah, the act of fleeing to God for shelter, and the psalm's opening posture of complete trust sets the tone for everything that follows.
Portion renders cheleq, drawn from the language of Israel's tribal land allotments; David applies this covenantal inheritance language directly to God Himself.

Holy One renders chasid, a title of covenant faithfulness; both Peter, Acts 2:27, and Paul, Acts 13:35, apply this verse directly to Christ, arguing that only His body, unlike David's, did not see decay.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 16:1-11. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of podcast.gospelinlife.com.
